coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/oxidized gum arabic-gelatin-based fluorescent probe (NAH) was prepared using gelatin (GL) and gum arabic (AG) biomolecules. The primary network structure of this hydrogel consisted of polyacrylamide (PAM), while a secondary network structure was constructed between oxidized gum arabic and gelatin through the reaction of the Schiff base, which significantly enhanced the mechanical properties, the stress and strain of NAH reached 266.47 KPa and 2175.75 %. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with yellow fluorescence emission properties were synthesized by hydrothermal method using salicylic acid as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source. The fluorescent sensor obtained by compounding NCDs with hydrogel was able to selectively detect Cr(VI). The linear range of its detection was 0–90 μM, and the detection limit was 0.19 μM. The maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of the NAH hydrogel was 169.4 mg/g, and its adsorption isotherm conformed to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model, and the adsorption kinetics conformed to the quasi-secondary kinetic model. Compared with the NCDs solution, the NAH hydrogel showed superior comprehensive performance, especially in solving the problem of recovery of NCDs. Meanwhile, this hydrogel provides an effective insight into the efficient detection and adsorption of environmental pollutants.

Microstructural characterization of NCDs and NAH. (a) SEM of AGGA; (b) SEM of NAH; (c) FT-IR spectra of NCDs, AGGA and NAH; (d) XRD spectra of NCDs.
(a) XPS full spectrum of NCDs; (b) C1s, (C) N1s, (d) O1 corresponding high resolution spectra.

Fluorescence properties of NCDs and NAH. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs at different wavelengths; (b) Normalized UV–visible absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra and fluorescence emission spectra plots of NCDs; (inset: photographs of NCDs under sunlight and UV irradiation); (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of NAH at different wavelengths; (d) Normalized UV–visible absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra and fluorescence emission spectra; (inset: photographs of NAH under sunlight and UV irradiation).

Fluorescence stability test of NAH. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra in the pH 2–12 range (λex = 417 nm); the inset shows the corresponding histogram of fluorescence ratios; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra in the temperature range 30–80 (λex = 420 nm); (c) Collapsed folded line plots of fluorescence intensity ratios for a NaCl solution with a concentration of 0–0.5 M; (d) Fluorescence intensities of different times of irradiation with 365 nm UV light ratios for different times of UV irradiation at 365 nm.

Ion detection performance of NAH. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 417 nm) after addition of various metal ions to NAH; (b) Histograms of fluorescence response of NAH to various metal ions and subsequent addition of Cr (VI) NAH; (c) Optical photographs of NAH after addition of various metal ions to NAH under natural light; (d) Optical photographs of NAH after addition of various metal ions to NAH under UV light.

Selective sensitivity of NAH to Cr (VI). (a) Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 417 nm) of NAH after adding different concentrations of Cr (VI) (0–1000 μM) to NAH; (b) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of NAH versus Cr (VI) concentration.

Adsorption properties. (a) Adsorption isotherms of Cr(VI) by NAH and AGGA; (b) Adsorption kinetics of Cr(VI) by NAH and AGGA; (c) Results of the Langmuir model fit; (d) Results of the Freundlich model fit; (e) Results of the pseudo-primary model fit; (f) Results of the pseudo-secondary model fit.
Schematic diagram of adsorption and detection mechanism of Cr (VI) by NAH.
The sensing performance of different fluorescence sensors for the detection of Cr (VI) was reviewed.

head.ce:title: Construction of carbon dot-embedded fluorescent hydrogels based on oxidized gum arabic-gelatin Schiff base for Cr(VI) detection applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/oxidized gum arabic-gelatin-based fluorescent probe (NAH) was prepared using gelatin (GL) and gum arabic (AG) biomolecules. The primary network structure of this hydrogel consisted of polyacrylamide (PAM), while a secondary network structure was constructed between oxidized gum arabic and gelatin through the reaction of the Schiff base, which significantly enhanced the mechanical properties, the stress and strain of NAH reached 266.47 KPa and 2175.75 %. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with yellow fluorescence emission properties were synthesized by hydrothermal method using salicylic acid as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source. The fluorescent sensor obtained by compounding NCDs with hydrogel was able to selectively detect Cr(VI). The linear range of its detection was 0–90 μM, and the detection limit was 0.19 μM. The maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of the NAH hydrogel was 169.4 mg/g, and its adsorption isotherm conformed to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model, and the adsorption kinetics conformed to the quasi-secondary kinetic model. Compared with the NCDs solution, the NAH hydrogel showed superior comprehensive performance, especially in solving the problem of recovery of NCDs. Meanwhile, this hydrogel provides an effective insight into the efficient detection and adsorption of environmental pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are metals with a density of 4.5 g/cm3 or more, including mainly gold, silver, manganese, chromium, iron and lead [1]. Since heavy metal ions are toxic and cannot be degraded into non-toxic compounds in nature, they will continue to accumulate and transfer when they enter the environment, ultimately having a great impact on the environment and human health [2]. Chromium is found in nature mainly in the form of Cr(III) and Cr (VI), Cr(III) is a trace element essential to the human body and is basically harmless to the human body. Cr(VI) generally comes from industrial wastewater from industries such as electroplating, dyeing and chromium salts [3], and generally exists in the form of CrO4 2− and Cr2O7 2− ions in the water column. Compared with Cr(III), Cr(VI) is 100 times more toxic than Cr(III). Cr (VI)is easily absorbed into the body, causing inflammation after skin contact, diarrhoea, ulcers and other diseases through the digestive tract, and long-term or short-term exposure to Cr(VI) poses a carcinogenic risk [4].

Currently, the methods for detecting Cr(VI) in water include inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) [5], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [6], atomic absorption spectrophotometry, electrochemical analysis [7] and so on. However, these techniques generally require the use of large, expensive instruments and equipment, and have certain requirements for operators, which are not conducive to on-site detection and analysis, and impose great limitations on detection. Therefore it is attractive to develop a reliable, convenient, rapid and economical fluorescent sensor for detecting Cr(VI) in water bodies [8]. Carbon dots (CDs) have great potential in the field of fluorescent probes due to their unique optical properties, biocompatibility, photostability, low toxicity and ease of surface functionalization [9]. Currently, there are many reports on carbon spot detection of Cr(VI) being reported, For example, Ado et al. synthesized N, S-doped fluorescent carbon dots for the detection of Cr(VI) in the environment by microwave method with high sensitivity and a detection limit of 0.32 ng/m3 [10]. Zhang et al. synthesized N-BCD using citric acid and 1,2-diaminobenzene as raw materials by microwave method, which showed good sensitivity for Cr(VI) in the range of 0–60 mg/L with a detection limit of 0.1574 μg/L [11]. Goswami et al. used lignocellulosic biomass (banana stems) and phosphoric acid as raw materials to synthesize a carbon-dot fluorescent sensor by a hydrothermal method and used it for the detection of Cr (VI), which was detected in a linear detection range of 10–30 μM with a limit of detection of 2.4 μM [12]. However, these assays use a single aqueous solution of CDs as the standard indicator, and these CDs will gradually accumulate over the testing time, which will bias the measurement results, as well as make it difficult to recover the carbon points after testing [13].

To solve this problem, immobilizing CDs in hydrogels is a very feasible solution [14]. Hydrogels are three-dimensional (3D) mesh structures [15–18], which can provide transparency, semi-wettability, simple processing and have certain physical properties [19], in addition to the high specific surface area of hydrogels and the abundance of functional groups on the surface to better accommodate CDs [20]. Combining hydrogels with CDs not only confers better dispersion of CDs and avoids fluorescence quenching ACQ due to the aggregation of CDs [21], but also CDs bring more active sites to the hydrogel to enhance the adsorption performance of the hydrogel [22]. There have been some recent reports on combining carbon dots with hydrogels. For example, Yuan et al. synthesized a novel cellulose nanofibre‑carbon dot fluorescent lignin hydrogel. The hydrogel showed high sensitivity to Cr (VI) with a detection limit of 11.2 mg/L, a linear range of 15–200 mg/L and a maximum adsorption capacity of 599.9 mg/g [23]. Zeng et al. prepared chitosan (CS)/carbon-dot grafted cellulose nanocrystals (CNCD) composite hydrogel, which showed strong adsorption capacity and sensitive detection of Cr(VI), with a maximum adsorption capacity of 217.8 mg/g [24]. Luo et al. successfully synthesized carbon dots (CD)-modified NPs) and cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) in combination with amino-functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles (AF-Fe3O4 by cross-linking. Quantitative detection of Cr(VI) was achieved in the linear range of 20–800 mg/L [25]. Gum arabic (AG), a common polysaccharide, is often used as a raw material for making hydrogels, and the abundance of hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and other functional groups on its surface endow it with the characteristics of easy modification and abundant hydrogen bond donor-acceptor [26], so we believe that gum arabic is a promising substrate, and the combination of it with CDs has a great potential for development [27].

In this study, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/oxidized gum arabic-gelatin-based fluorescent probe (NAH) was prepared using gelatin and gum arabic biomolecules. The primary network structure of this hydrogel consists of polyacrylamide (PAM), whereas a secondary network structure was constructed between the oxidized gum arabic and gelatin by the reaction of the Schiff base, which significantly enhanced the mechanical properties of the hydrogel. Tests showed that the stress and strain of NAH reached 266.47 KPa and 2175.75 %, respectively, and the structural integrity was effectively maintained during the detection process. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with yellow fluorescence emission characteristics were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using salicylic acid as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source. The fluorescent sensor obtained by compositing NCDs with hydrogels was able to selectively detect Cr (VI) based on the fluorescence enhancement effect. The groups on the surface of NAH are easily coordinated with metal ions, and the ligand-metal charge-transfer leaps and intramolecular charge-transfer effects occur between NAH and Cr (VI), and the fluorescence enhancement of the former can be observed, which achieves the specific detection of Cr (VI), and the application of NAH to the detection of Cr (VI) in the actual aqueous environment, also with good reproducibility. Due to the excellent adsorption capacity of the hydrogel, it showed significant advantages especially for the detection of Cr (VI). When detecting Cr (VI), the fluorescence signal of the hydrogel was enhanced, which effectively reduced the background signal and significantly improved the accuracy of detection. The linear range was 0–90 μM, and the detection limit was 0.19 μM. The morphology, chemical composition, optical properties and adsorption characteristics of the hydrogel were systematically characterized, and the results showed that the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of the NAH hydrogel was 169.4 mg/g, and its adsorption isotherm conformed to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model, and the adsorption kinetics conformed to the quasi-secondary kinetic model. The effects of initial concentration and contact time of Cr (VI) on the adsorption effect of NAH were also systematically investigated to analyze the mechanism of its efficient adsorption and detection. Compared with the simple NCDs solution, the NAH hydrogel showed superior comprehensive performance, especially in solving the problem of difficult recovery of NCDs, which provides an effective way. Meanwhile, based on the easily degradable oxidized gum arabic-gelatin system, the hydrogel has a promising application in environmental analysis providing effective insights into the efficient detection and adsorption treatment of environmental pollutants.

Salicylic acid (Sa), o-phenylenediamine (O-PD), gum arabic (GA; pharmaceutical grade; Aladdin China); gelatin (GL; 99.5 %; Aladdin China); acrylamide (AM; 99.0 %; Aladdin China); sodium periodate (NaIO4; 99.5 %; Aladdin China); potassium persulfate (KPS; 99.5 %; Aladdin; China), N,N,N'N′-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED; 99.0 %; Aladdin; China) sodium hydroxide (NaOH; 97 %; Aladdin; China), hydrochloric acid (HCl; 99 %; Beijing Chemical Reagent; China). Sodium chloride (NaCl, average Mw 58.44) was purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory. The chemicals, reagents, and substrates used in this study were of analytical grade and were used as is without any additional purification, and all experiments were performed using ultrapure water obtained using a Milli-Q purification system (Merck, Germany).

0.1 g of salicylic acid and 0.05 g of o-phenylenediamine were weighed and dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water with stirring. Subsequently, it was added to a 50 mL PTFE-lined autoclave reactor and hydrothermally reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. The dark yellow solution obtained was naturally cooled to room temperature. The resulting liquid was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the NCDs solution was obtained by removing large particles and impurities through a 0.22 μM ion filter. The NCDs solution was freeze-dried into powder and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of oxidized gum arabic aldehyde (AG)
ce:section.ce:para: 5 g of gum arabic was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and mixed with 5 mL of sodium periodate solution at a concentration of 0.01 mol/L. The reaction was carried out in the dark. The mixed solution was kept at 20 °C in the dark and stirred magnetically for 6 h. After the reaction was completed, the liquid was transferred to a dialysis bag and dialyzed in distilled water for 48 h. Silver nitrate solution was added dropwise to the dialyzed deionized water to detect whether all the sodium periodate was removed, and dialysis was completed if there was no precipitation. The solution fluid in the dialysis band was frozen and freeze-dried to obtain oxidized arabinogalactan (AG) sealed and dried for storage.

Arabic-gelatin-acrylamide hydrogel (AGGA) were prepared by adjusting different proportions of oxidized gum arabic aldehyde (AG), gelatin (GL) and acrylamide (AM). A series of AG with different contents were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and the solution was transferred to a water bath at 60 °C. Different contents of gelatin were added to the reactor and after complete dissolution, 1 mL of hydrochloric acid solution (pH = 3) was added. 5 g of acrylamide was added after 3 h. After adding potassium persulfate and 20 μL of N,N,N‘,N'-tetramethylethylenediamine, the mixture was injected into a glass mould with a silicon isolation layer and reacted at 50 °C for 6 h to make hydrogel gels strips with a length of 4 cm, a width of 1.5 cm, and a thickness of 2 mm, which were named as AG1G5A,AG2G4A, AG3G3A,AG4G2A,AG5G1A, tested their mechanical properties, and selected the hydrogel with the best mechanical properties for subsequent testing.

The AGGA gels were dried in a blast drying oven at 50 °C and set aside. A certain mass of NCDs powder was dissolved in deionized water to form a CDs impregnation solution, and the AGGA hydrogels were immersed in NCDs impregnation solution with different concentrations (0.25 mg/mL, 0.5 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL, 1.25 mg/mL, 1.5 mg/mL, 1.75 mg/mL, 2 mg/mL) for 5 h. The made hydrogels were named NAH-1 to NAH-8.

To study the Cr (VI) selectivity of NAH with respect to other metal ions. Solutions of different metal ions: Ag(I), Ca(II), K(I), Hg(II), Fe(II), Mg(II), Cu(II), Pb(II), Na(I), Cr(III), Cr (VI), Ni(II), Zn(II), Co(II), Al(III), and Fe(III) were prepared at a concentration of 0.1 M in order to study the change in fluorescence intensity of the hydrogels. The hydrogels were immersed in different metal ion solutions for 4 h to solubility equilibrium, and their fluorescence intensity changes were measured at the optimum excitation wavelength of 417 nm, and the color changes produced visually under 365 nm UV and natural light irradiation were observed and photographed for preservation, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of standard additions of Cr (VI) in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the application of NAH in real water samples, spiked recovery method was used to determine lake water, tap water and bottled mineral water. Tap water and mineral water were obtained from the laboratory and local supermarkets, respectively, and could be used directly without any pretreatment. The lake water samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was collected and filtered through 0.22 μM filter membrane as the samples to be tested. NAH was immersed in actual water samples with different concentrations of Cr (VI) and the fluorescence intensity was measured, and the recoveries and deviations were calculated separately.

Batch adsorption experiments were carried out on NAH fluorescent hydrogels and AGGA hydrogels to investigate the adsorption properties of the prepared hydrogels. NAH (2 g) and Cr (VI) ion solution (100 mL) were added to a 250 mL flask. Then, the flask was placed in a water bath shaker at 200 rpm speed. The adsorption temperature was maintained at 25 °C. In these batch experiments, the effects of different factors were examined: different Cr (VI) concentrations (20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 mg/L) at a fixed contact time (240 min) and different contact times (30, 60, 90, 120, 150, and 180 mg/L) at a fixed initial concentration (100 mg/L), 90, 120, 150, 180, 210 and 240 min) at a fixed initial concentration (100 mg/L). The residual Cr (VI) concentration in solution was determined spectrophotometrically using dibenzoyl dihydrazide.

The internal structure of NCDs was investigated using a SmartLab X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan), and the elements in the NCDs were analyzed using a JSM-5610LV X-ray spectrometer (Electron, Japan). The surface of the prepared hydrogels was analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Phenom Prox, USA). The prepared hydrogels were ruptured in liquid nitrogen, and then the ruptured surfaces were coated with gold and examined using a high voltage of 15 kV. UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured using a Cary 5000 UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer (Agilent, USA). Surface functional groups were analyzed using a Nicolet IS50 Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) analysis was performed using an F97Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Prism, Shanghai, China). In addition, AGGA and NAH were tested in tensile and compression on a universal testing machine (Shimadzu, Japan).

The process of NAH synthesis is depicted in Fig. 1 The NAH synthesis pathway consists of three main components. In the first step, gum arabic was oxidized to gum arabic aldehyde with sodium periodate, and the oxidized gum arabic aldehyde was reacted with gelatin to form GAA-GL by Schiff's base reaction at 60 °C under acidic conditions at pH = 3. GAA-GL was then thermally initiated to free-radical polymerization with acrylamide to form AGGA [28]. In the second step NCDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis using salicylic acid as carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as nitrogen source [29]. In the third step AGGA was loaded with NCDs by impregnation embedding method. Fig. S1a-b, depicts the fluorescence behavior of NAH with different additions of NCDs, where it is observed that NAH appears yellow in natural light. NAH has a bright yellow fluorescence emission under UV irradiation at 365 nm, and the fluorescence intensity increases and then decreases with the content of NCDs, among which NAH-2 exhibits the highest fluorescence intensity. From the fluorescence emission spectra of NAH with different concentrations of NCDs content added in Fig. S1b, it can be seen that the highest fluorescence intensity of NAH-2 was observed at 567 nm with a quantum yield of 20.3 %. Subsequent tests of NAH were prepared according to the material ratio of NAH-2.

Gum Arabic oxide-gelatin-acrylamide (AGGA) composite hydrogels were synthesized by free radical polymerization. The mechanical properties of the hydrogels with different ratios were investigated by adjusting the ratios of gum arabic oxide (AG) and gelatin (GL) in the hydrogels to explore the optimal ratio of the two. Mechanical properties of hydrogels with different ratios, i.e. AG1G5A, AG2G4A, AG3G3A, AG4G2A, AG5G1A. The analysis by tensile test is shown in Fig. 2A that as the gum arabic oxide content in the hydrogel increases to x = 1.6 wt%, 3.3 wt%, 5 wt% and 6.67 wt%, and the fracture stress gradually increased with 29.59, 95.76, 153.9 and 266.47 KPa and the strain increased with 1043.44, 1163.55, 1548.99 and 2175.75 %, respectively. When the content of gum arabic oxide increased to 8.33 %, the mechanics of the hydrogel started to deteriorate, mainly due to the increase in the content of gum arabic oxide and the viscosity of the hydrogel, which led to the deterioration of the mechanics. The elastic modulus and toughness of the hydrogel are depicted in Fig. 2b. The fracture toughness of the hydrogel reaches 78.391 KJ/m3 when the content of gum arabic aldehyde oxide reaches 6.67 wt%, this is due to the fact that the rise in the content of gum arabic aldehyde oxide can provide more binding sites for chemical cross-linking, which results in a significant enhancement of the fracture stress and strain of the hydrogel. The fatigue resistance of AG4G2A hydrogel was investigated by continuous tensile tests, and the stress-strain curves are shown in Fig. 2c. The hydrogels were subjected to 10 cycles of stretching under 100 % deformation conditions, and a clear hysteresis return line could be observed during the first loading and unloading cycle, but there was essentially no difference between the nine curves during the subsequent nine loading and unloading cycles. This is due to the fact that the inhomogeneous structure within the hydrogel forms a stable and homogeneous structure after the first loading-unloading cycle. As a result, the subsequent nine loading-unloading curves almost overlap. Fig. 2d responds to the cyclic mechanics of the hydrogel during stretching, and the stress level basically remains above 87.39 % with the increase in the number of cycles, confirming the good fatigue resistance of the AG4G2A hydrogel. For the above tests on the mechanical properties of hydrogels we selected the AG4G2A hydrogel with the best mechanical properties as the subsequent experimental hydrogel AGGA. In addition, the mechanical properties of the NAH hydrogel after the composite of AGGA hydrogels with NCDs were tested as shown in Fig. S2. From the tensile stress-strain curves of NAH and AGGA in Fig. S2a, it can be seen that NAH fabricated from AGGA-composite NCDs exhibits excellent mechanical properties, with a fracture stress of 293 KPa and an elongation at break of 798 %. The compression curve of the hydrogel as shown in Fig. S2b shows that the NAH doped with NCDs has better compressive strength. The results showed that after AGGA hydrogels were doped with NCDs, a large number of hydrogen bonds between NCDs and the hydrogel surface may exist to enhance the mechanical properties of the hydrogels, and the excellent mechanical properties provide a possibility for their further applications.

The microstructures inside the AGGA hydrogel and NAH were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) as shown in Fig. 3a-b, both of which have a continuous porous structure inside. The pore size of NAH was slightly smaller than that of AGGA hydrogel, probably because of the additional hydrogen bonding between the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of NCDs and the amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of AGGA hydrogel, which slightly reduced the pore size, and this kind of structure improves the mechanical properties of the hydrogel as well as provides a good reaction environment for the subsequent detection of Cr (VI).

The FT-IR spectra of NAH, AGGA, and NCDs are shown in Fig. 3c, and the absorption peaks of NCDs at 1619 cm−1 and 1490 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, respectively [30]. The absorption peak of NCDs at 1387 cm−1 is a CN stretching vibration. These confirm the presence of a large number of NH, COOH on the surface of NCDs, and these groups confer good hydrophilicity to the NCDs while also providing a number of hydrogen-bonded ligands for complexation with AGGA hydrogels. The broadening of the NAH peak around 3400 cm−1 compared to the AGGA hydrogel suggests that there is a large amount of hydrogen bonding between the NH, OH and other groups on the surface of the carbon quantum dots and the OH of the NAH hydrogel [31]. The characteristic absorption peak of NCDs at around 3200 cm−1 is the aromatic CH absorption peak, and the absorption peak of NAH becomes stronger here compared to AGGA hydrogels, which is due to the increased NH absorption peak after doping with carbon dots [32]. The characteristic absorption peak at 2922 cm−1 for NAH and AGGA hydrogels is due to the CH stretching vibration. The characteristic absorption peak at 1660 cm−1 is the CN/CO stretching vibration peak of NAH with amide in AGGA hydrogel. The position and peak shape of the two characteristic peaks do not change greatly from the image, which confirms that the NCD and AGGA hydrogel are bonded to each other by the interaction force of hydrogen bonding. [33]

The size and morphology of NCDs were investigated by TEM, as shown in Fig. S3, the prepared NCDs are close to spherical in the image, the average diameter is at 7.8 nm, and the particles are mainly distributed between 5 and 10 nm have good dispersion. The crystal structure of NCDs was analyzed by x-ray diffraction (XRD), and as shown in Fig. 3d, NCDs have a broadened peak at 20.2°, which is typical of the amorphous carbon structure of carbon dots [34]. This is consistent with the structure characterized by the tem of NCDs in Fig. 1. In summary, the FT-IR, XRD analyses indicate the successful preparation of NAH.

In order to gain a deeper understanding of the elemental composition and chemical state of NCDs X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to characterize them. The total XPS spectrum of NCDs is shown in Fig. 4a, showing three typical peaks C1 s (284.94 eV), N1s (399.0 2 eV) and O 1 s (532.35 eV) [35]. The high-resolution spectra of the three elements were then processed by peak fitting. As shown in Fig. 4b C1s spectrogram at 284.35 eV (CC/CC), 285.15 eV (CN/CO), 285.95 eV (CO), and 288.4 eV (CCO) four bands appear [36]. Fig. 4c N1s spectrum after deconvolution shows three peaks at 398.85 eV (Pyridinic N), 400.0 eV (Amino N), and 400.45 eV (Graphitic N) representing Pyridinic N, Amino N, and Graphitic N, respectively [37]. Fig. 4d O 1 s spectrum is divided into two peaks, 531.0 eV (CO) and 532.5 eV (CO) [38]. These xps results are consistent with the FT-IR analysis, and in summary, a large amount of OH,NH2,COOH exists on the surface of NCDs. XRD, FT-IR and XPS analyses showed successful preparation of NCDs as previously described.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of NCDs and NAHs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to better investigate the optical properties of NCDs solutions with NAH, the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of both at specific concentrations and environments were tested. The fluorescence spectra of the NCDs solutions are shown in Fig. 5a, where the fluorescence intensity corresponding to the emission wavelength increases and then decreases at the excitation wavelengths from 390 nm to 450 nm. It is noteworthy that the position of the emission peak of NCDs has been 567 nm during the regular increase and decrease of the fluorescence intensity, and the position of the emission peak has not been blue-shifted or red-shifted, indicating that there is no excitation dependence of the NCDs, which may be related to the particle size and the molecular state of the surface of the carbon quantum dots. The maximum emission wavelength of NCDs is observed in Fig. 5b at the optimal excitation of 417 nm at 567 nm with a broad Stoke shift [39]. And there is an absorption peak at 430 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NCDs, which is likely to originate from the Π-Π* leaps of the aromatic Sp2 structural domains [40]. The inset of Fig. 5b shows on the left the color of the NCDs solution in natural light as a pale yellow color, and on the right is an image of the NCDs solution under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, with the solution emitting a bright yellow fluorescence. We similarly analyzed the fluorescence of NAH, and the photoluminescence profile of NAH in Fig. 5c shows an optimal excitation wavelength of 417 nm and an optimal emission wavelength of 570 nm, with a slight redshift compared to NCDs, which may be due to the photoelectron-induced effect (PET) [41]. The maximum excitation wavelength of the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NAH at 427 nm is close to the absorption band at 430 nm of the NCDs solution, suggesting that the emission comes from the Π-Π* leaps of aromatic Sp2 in NCDs [42]. The comparison of fluorescence intensity between NCDs and NAH in the same environment is shown in Fig. S3. The optimal emission wavelengths of both NAH and NCDs solutions at an excitation wavelength of 417 nm are at 567 nm, but the fluorescence intensity of NAH is 4.18 times higher than that of NCDs solution. This is due to the formation of a large number of hydrogen bonds between the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of the carbon dots and the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the molecular chain of the hydrogel after the binding of the NCDs with the hydrogel, which endowed the NCDs with good dispersion and avoided the aggregation bursting effect (ACQ) generated by the aggregated state of the NCDs to enhance the fluorescence intensity of the NAH [43]. In summary, we can learn that the fluorescence properties of NAH are inherited from NCDs and have been enhanced to a certain extent, which greatly enhances the potential for subsequent applications.

Evaluating the stability of a fluorescent sensor under different conditions is a critical step in ensuring the reliability of the sensor in real-world applications. For this reason, we carried out stability tests on NCDs and NAH by observing the fluorescence intensities of NCDs and NAH at different pH values, different temperatures, different NaCl salt solution concentrations, and exposure to different simultaneous UV irradiation durations, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence performance of NAH was basically stable under different PH (4–12) environments. The fluorescence intensity of NAH starts to quench when pH < 4, which may be related to the H+ concentration in the environment, and a high H+ concentration may cause the COOH and OH present on the N surface to be protonated, which in turn alters the surface state of the NCDs, and the fluorescence intensity consequently diminishes [44]. As in Fig. 6b we continued to test the fluorescence emission intensity of NAH 30 °C–80 °C, with the increase of temperature NAH gradually decreased the fluorescence intensity at 567 nm, it may be that NAH follows the Boltzmann distribution, and the increase of temperature leads to the change of the surface state of the NCDs, and the non-radiative channels on the surface are activated, and a large number of excited electrons are returned to the ground state through the non-radiative process, which leads to the decrease of the fluorescence intensity. This also confirms, on the other hand, that the fluorescence behavior of NAH mainly originates from impregnated embedded NCDs [45]. We then tested the fluorescence stability of NAH in different concentrations of NaCl solutions, as shown in Fig. 6c, where NAH was immersed in 0–0.5 M NaCl solution for 5 min, and there was no significant change in the PL intensity of NAH. It can be seen that it has good salt resistance and can perform fluorescence detection in samples with high salinity. We put NAH under the UV lamp of 360 nm for 60 min, and tested its fluorescence intensity every 10 min, and the results are shown in Fig. 6d. The fluorescence intensity of NAH is basically maintained. This indicates that it has good resistance to UV light [46]. In conclusion, it shows that our prepared NAH has good fluorescence stability, and NAH has the potential to become as a fluorescence sensor.

In this study, the selectivity of NAH for different metal ions (Ag(I), Ca(II), K(I), Hg(II), Fe(II), Mg(II), Cu(II), Pb(II), Na(I), Cr(III), Cr(VI), Ni(II), Zn(II), Co(II), Al(III), and Fe(III).) was investigated. NAH was immersed in 5 ml of different metal ion solutions at a concentration of (10 μM) and the effect of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of NAH was investigated at the optimum excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 7a, Cr(VI) can significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity of NAH compared with other heavy metals, and the corresponding emission intensities of the other metal ions at the optimal excitation wavelengths after 2 h of mixed immersion with NAH, respectively, showed small changes. This indicates that NAH has good selectivity for the metal Cr(VI). To further test the specific detection of Cr(VI) by NAH, we immersed NAH in different metal ion solutions before adding 100 μL Cr(VI) ion solution, and the results were shown in Fig. 7b, NAH fluorescence intensity changed very little (shown by black bars). The fluorescence intensity of NAH was significantly enhanced by the addition of Cr(VI) ion solution again (grey bars in the figure). In summary, NAH has good specificity and anti-interference ability for Cr(VI).

When NAH detects Cr(VI) ions, the fluorescence enhancement of NAH is accompanied by a slight red shift of the emission wavelength, which may be due to the fluorescence enhancement induced by intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), as can be seen from the UV absorption spectra of NAH after the addition of Cr(VI) in Fig. S5.The UV absorption wavelengths of NAH are changed and red-shifted after the addition of Cr(VI) ions and, according to Fig. S6 the change of wave peaks in the fingerprint region on the infrared spectrum of NAH containing Cr(VI) ions, it can be speculated that a new chemical bond was formed between Cr(VI) and the electron-donating groups on the surface of NAH, and the electron transfer of Cr(VI) to NAH caused a change in its fluorescence intensity. From this we hypothesize that the mechanism by which Cr(VI) enhances NAH fluorescence is the ICT effect [47].

To further test the sensitivity of NAH to Cr(VI) selection, we immersed NAH in Cr(VI) solutions of different concentrations (0–1000 μM) for 3 h and observed its fluorescence intensity. The results are shown in Fig. 8a, where the fluorescence intensity of NAH is enhanced with the concentration of Cr(VI). As the concentration of Cr(VI) rises, the PL emission response of NAH under 360 nm excitation changes from pale yellow to bright yellow. In addition, the concentration of Cr(VI) was in the range of 0–90 μM, and the relative intensity of fluorescence had a good linear relationship with it, which conformed to the best-fit equation y = 0.00580× + 0.26616 (R2 = 0.9968) The limit of detection (LOD) of Cr(VI) response was 0.19 μM, The limit of detection was lower and the linear range was wider than that previously reported for other different precursor CD determinations of Cr(VI). Comparison of NAH with various published materials is shown in Table 1 . In conclusion, this shows that NAH has good sensitivity to Cr(VI) and can be used as a good sensor for detecting Cr(VI).

To explore the practical application of NAH in the detection of Cr(VI) ions in real water samples, we used laboratory tap water, local lake water and mineral water as three different water samples to be tested. The spiked recovery method was used to detect the Cr(VI) content in it. The results are shown in Table 2 , the average recoveries of Cr(VI) were 100.30 %, 99.67 % and 100.07 % in lake water, 99.57 %, 99.78 % and 99.87 % in mineral water and 100.23 %, 99.50 % and 100.01 % in laboratory tap water. The relative standard deviations of Cr(VI) detection in real water samples can be controlled below 1.7 %, and the above results indicate that NAH as a fluorescent probe has high accuracy and stability in detecting Cr(VI) ions in environmental samples, and has high potential for application [57].

The adsorption experiments on hydrogels were carried out at 25 °C, and the adsorption data of Cr(VI) by NAH and AGGA at different initial concentrations are shown in Fig. 9a. The adsorption of Cr(VI) by NAH and AGGA increased gradually with the higher initial concentration of Cr(VI) in the solution, and the equilibrium adsorption of NAH was always larger than the equilibrium adsorption of AGGA at the same C0. The adsorption data of NAH were fitted using isotherms in Fig. 9c-d, and from the fitting results the coefficient of determination of Langmuir isotherm (R2 = 0.99157) was higher than the coefficient of determination of Freundlich isotherm (R2 = 0.98859), and the maximum adsorption capacity of NAH was 169.43 mg/g. Therefore, it is shown that this adsorption is monolayer adsorption [58]. As shown in Table 1, comparing the maximum adsorption capacity of this hydrogel with the maximum adsorption capacity of other carbon-dotted hydrogels, the NAH hydrogel has a higher adsorption capacity for Cr(VI).

The effect of adsorption time on the adsorption capacity is shown in Fig. 9b.The adsorption of Cr(VI) ions by NAH gradually reached equilibrium at 200 min and then increased slowly. The adsorption capacity of NAH was greater than that of AGGA at the same contact time. The adsorption data of NAH were analyzed with the proposed primary and proposed secondary kinetic models and the results obtained are shown in Fig. S9e, 9 f. Combined with the fitting results the R2 of the proposed secondary kinetic model is higher than that of the proposed primary kinetic model, which indicates that the adsorption process of NAH on Cr(VI) is a chemisorption process [59].

Combined with these experimental results, the adsorption and detection mechanism of Cr(VI) ions on NAH hydrogel was proposed, as shown in Fig. 10 , the porous structure of NAH hydrogel and the internal NCDs provide a large number of ion transport channels and active sites for Cr(VI) ions, and Cr(VI) ions are immobilized inside the hydrogel when they enter the interior, so that the hydrogel has excellent adsorption properties. And a part of Cr(VI) ions were immobilized on the surface of NCDs to interact, leading to the fluorescence enhancement of the carbon dots, in conjunction with the hydrogel, while allowing good detection and adsorption properties of NAH [60].

In this study, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/oxidized gum arabic-gelatin-based fluorescent probe (NAH) was prepared using gelatin and gum arabic biomolecules. The primary network structure of this hydrogel consists of polyacrylamide (PAM), whereas a secondary network structure was constructed between the oxidized gum arabic and gelatin by the reaction of the Schiff base, which significantly enhanced the mechanical properties of the hydrogel. Tests showed that the NAH was effective in maintaining structural integrity during testing by reaching 266.47 KPa and 2175.75 % stress and strain, respectively. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with yellow fluorescence emission properties were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using salicylic acid as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source. The fluorescent sensor obtained by compositing NCDs with hydrogels was able to selectively detect Cr(VI) based on the fluorescence enhancement effect. The groups on the surface of NAH are easily coordinated with metal ions, and the ligand-metal charge-transfer leaps and intramolecular charge-transfer effects occur between NAH and Cr(VI), and the fluorescence enhancement of the former can be observed, which achieves the specific detection of Cr(VI), and the application of NAH to the detection of Cr(VI) in the actual aqueous environment, also with good reproducibility. Due to the excellent adsorption capacity of the hydrogel, it showed significant advantages especially for the detection of Cr(VI). When detecting Cr(VI), the fluorescence signal of the hydrogel was enhanced, which effectively reduced the background signal and significantly improved the accuracy of detection. The linear range of detection was 0–90 μM, and the detection limit was 0.19 μM. The morphology, chemical composition, optical properties and adsorption characteristics of the hydrogel were systematically characterized, and the results showed that the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of the NAH hydrogel was 169.4 mg/g, and its adsorption isotherm conformed to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model, and its adsorption kinetics conformed to the quasi-two level kinetic model. The effects of initial concentration and contact time of Cr(VI) on the adsorption effect of NAH were also systematically investigated to analyze the mechanism of its efficient adsorption and detection. Compared with the simple NCDs solution, the NAH hydrogel showed superior comprehensive performance, especially in solving the problem of difficult recovery of NCDs. Meanwhile, based on the easily degradable oxidized gum arabic-gelatin system, this hydrogel has a promising application in environmental analysis and provides an effective insight into the efficient detection and adsorption of environmental pollutants.
